ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'rice milk' class (FOODON_03302050); no specific flavoured/sweetened variant code exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJW covers rice drink / rice milk generically. No flavoured-variant sub-code available; closest parent match documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for flavoured rice milk as of 2026. Null used rather than invent a code.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of flavoured rice milk is one glass of approximately 240 ml (240 g), providing around 137 kcal, 28.3 g carbohydrate, and 19.3 g sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Flavoured Rice Milk — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F01020007 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status 'reviewed'. All per_100g values are taken from this source without modification.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Rice milk (plain, unsweetened) has a published GI of approximately 86 (high) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The flavoured/sweetened variant is expected to have an equal or higher GI due to added sugar; GI 86 is used as a conservative lower-bound estimate. Glycemic load per 240 g serving = (86 × 11.8 / 100) × 2.4 = 24.3 g. The input record's embedded GI value of 31 (low) is inconsistent with all published evidence for rice milk and has been overridden. Rice milk is consistently reported as one of the highest-GI plant milks in peer-reviewed literature.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content (8.05 g/100 g) in a sweetened beverage independently elevates blood pressure risk; sodium is moderate at 52 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: People with hypertension should avoid sweetened rice milk due to its high added-sugar load (8.05 g/100 g), which independently raises cardiovascular and blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines; choose unsweetened plant milks with sodium below 100 mg/100 g instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~86), high sugar (8.05 g/100 g), and low dietary fibre (0.41 g/100 g) cause rapid postprandial glucose excursions
type2_diabetes.guidance: Flavoured rice milk is unsuitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high GI (~86), high added-sugar content (8.05 g/100 g), and negligible fibre (0.41 g/100 g), all of which drive rapid blood glucose spikes; unsweetened soy or almond milk are strongly preferred alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added-sugar content (8.05 g/100 g) in a liquid form raises triglyceride and inflammatory risk; fat content is low (1.11 g/100 g) and saturated fat is negligible
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While flavoured rice milk is low in total fat and saturated fat, its high added-sugar content (8.05 g/100 g) consumed as a beverage raises triglyceride levels and inflammatory markers relevant to cardiovascular disease risk; limit intake and substitute with unsweetened plant milks.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High added sugar (8.05 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; food provides no anti-inflammatory nutrients (no omega-3, no polyphenols, negligible fibre)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Flavoured rice milk is not recommended for people with rheumatoid arthritis because its high added-sugar content promotes systemic inflammation and it provides no compensating anti-inflammatory nutrients; consider unsweetened soy milk (isoflavones) or walnut milk (omega-3) as alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Flavoured rice milk is a commercially produced, dairy-free, plant-based beverage made by blending milled rice with water and adding flavourings and sugar. It has a thin, watery consistency with a mildly sweet taste and a subtle rice flavour, enhanced by added flavouring agents. Per 100 g it provides 57 kcal, 11.8 g carbohydrate (of which 8.05 g is sugar), 0.41 g protein, 1.11 g fat, 0.41 g dietary fibre, and 52 mg sodium. Calcium and iron are reported as 0 mg/100 g in the HPB Singapore database, indicating this product is not fortified. The glycemic index of rice milk is approximately 86 (high), making it one of the highest-GI plant milks available; the flavoured and sweetened variant is clinically unsuitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing blood glucose. The high added-sugar content (8.05 g/100 g) also raises concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease through triglyceride elevation and inflammatory pathways. Rice milk is naturally gluten-free, vegan, and dairy-free, making it a common allergen-friendly milk substitute, but its poor nutritional profile — low protein, negligible fibre, high GI, and high sugar — limits its clinical utility compared to unsweetened soy or almond milk. It is classified as ultra-processed and is not recommended as a daily beverage for individuals with metabolic conditions.
common_user_queries: Is rice milk good for diabetics?
Rice milk vs almond milk vs soy milk
Can I drink rice milk if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F01020007, review_status 'reviewed', confidence 0.95). GI value corrected from the embedded database entry (GI 31, which is inconsistent with all published evidence) to GI 86 based on University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed data for rice milk. All other values preserved from the approved HPB source.
notes: 1. SATURATED FAT: saturated_fat_g is set to 0.0 — rice milk contains negligible saturated fat; confirmed consistent with USDA FoodData Central rice milk entries (SR Legacy). 2. CALCIUM and IRON: calcium_mg and iron_mg are set to 0.0 as reported by HPB Singapore for this specific product (cr_id F01020007); this product appears to be unfortified. 3. CHOLESTEROL: cholesterol_mg set to 0.0 — plant-based beverage, naturally contains no cholesterol. 4. FIELDS SET TO -1: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific flavoured rice milk product are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for F01020007 and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for a directly matching flavoured rice milk product as of 2026. 5. GI CORRECTION: The input record contained an embedded GI value of 31 (low) attributed to 'Embedded GI Database' with confidence 0.316. This value is inconsistent with all peer-reviewed evidence for rice milk, which consistently reports GI values of 79–92 (high). The embedded GI has been overridden with GI 86 from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). 6. MULTILINGUAL NAMES: This is a generic commercial product with no specific cultural or regional origin requiring mandatory multilingual naming under G6. Local name fields are set to null. 7. ONTOLOGY: FoodOn FOODON_03302050 (rice milk) and FoodEx2 A0EJW (rice drink) are used as closest_parent matches; no flavoured-variant-specific codes exist in either system.
